Row1.Rationale: In gefitinib‑sensitive NSCLC A549 cells, gefitinib decreases PI3K, AKT, p‑AKT, mTOR and p‑mTOR, inducing autophagy and apoptosis and inhibiting proliferation, whereas A549‑GR cells fail to downregulate this axis, linking pathway blockade to gefitinib sensitivity.

Row2.Rationale: In EGFR L858R/T790M H1975 cells, gefitinib alone incompletely suppresses EGFR‑PI3K/AKT‑mTOR signaling and tumor growth, but combined bufalin+gefitinib more strongly reduces EGFR, PI3K, AKT, mTOR and p70S6K expression and overcomes resistance, indicating persistent pathway activation drives gefitinib resistance.

Row3.Rationale: The PI3K/AKT pathway is a central RTK‑driven survival pathway in lung cancer; abnormal activation is a hallmark of tumor progression and therapy resistance, and multiple studies show that its sustained activity limits responses to EGFR‑TKIs including gefitinib.

Row4.Rationale: Gefitinib‑resistant PC9GR cells show higher AXL, p‑AKT and p‑ERK than parental PC9; enforced AXL overexpression increases gefitinib IC₅₀ ~3‑fold, while AXL knockdown restores sensitivity and suppresses PI3K/AKT and MAPK/ERK1/2 activation, implicating AXL‑driven bypass signaling in resistance.

Row5.Rationale: Gefitinib‑resistant HCC827‑GR cells exhibit marked loss of PHLPP; PHLPP knockdown in EGFR‑mutant cells preserves AKT and ERK1/2 phosphorylation despite EGFR inhibition, reduces gefitinib‑induced apoptosis, and increases IC₅₀, whereas PHLPP overexpression restores EGFR‑TKI sensitivity, showing that PHLPP loss reactivates dual AKT/ERK signaling and drives resistance.

Row6.Rationale: HDGF overexpression in initially gefitinib‑sensitive PC‑9 cells induces resistance and maintains Akt and ERK activation even when EGFR is inhibited; HDGF knockdown in resistant H1975 cells enhances gefitinib sensitivity, and Akt or ERK inhibitors (MK2206, U0126) blunt HDGF‑mediated resistance, indicating HDGF bypass‑activates EGFR downstream pathways.

Row7.Rationale: In primary‑resistant EGFR‑mutant H1975 cells, IGF‑1R signaling is markedly activated; pharmacologic IGF‑1R inhibition or siRNA knockdown enhances gefitinib‑induced growth inhibition and apoptosis via suppression of p‑AKT and upregulation of BIM, and metformin+gefitinib similarly suppresses IGF‑1R/AKT, implicating this pathway in intrinsic gefitinib resistance.

Row8.Pathway ID/Name: microRNA–PI3K/AKT axis (miR‑mediated modulation of EGFR‑TKI response)
Row8.Rationale: Multiple miRNAs modulate PI3K/AKT signaling and EGFR‑TKI response: miR‑200c enhances gefitinib sensitivity by blocking PI3K/AKT; miR‑135a induces gefitinib resistance through RAC1‑PI3K/AKT; let‑7c increases gefitinib sensitivity via RAS inhibition and PI3K/AKT and MEK/ERK inactivation, highlighting miRNA‑PI3K/AKT circuitry as a determinant of gefitinib response.

Row9.Rationale: Gefitinib induces autophagy in EGFR‑positive and EGFR‑wild‑type lung cancer cells via AMPK activation; in A549 cells, gefitinib‑triggered AMPK‑linked autophagy accompanies apoptosis and growth inhibition, while resistant A549‑GR cells fail to mount this response, suggesting intact AMPK–autophagy signaling contributes to gefitinib cytotoxicity.

Row10.Rationale: Blockade of PI3K/AKT/mTOR by gefitinib or LY294002 in A549 cells promotes autophagy and apoptosis, and pathway inactivation alone is sufficient to trigger cell death, indicating that suppression of this axis and induction of autophagy are key components of gefitinib sensitivity.

Row11.Pathway ID/Name: EGFR–shared PI3K/AKT pathway with other RTKs (IGF‑1R, FGFR)
Row11.Rationale: The PI3K/AKT pathway is activated downstream of multiple RTKs including EGFR, IGF‑1R and FGFR; in lung cancer, bypass activation of PI3K/AKT by non‑EGFR RTKs (e.g., IGF‑1R in H1975, HDGF‑driven signaling) maintains survival signaling when EGFR is inhibited, mediating resistance to gefitinib.

Row12.Pathway ID/Name: EGFR–PI3K/AKT–mTOR axis targeted by bufalin + gefitinib
Row12.Rationale: In H1975 xenografts and cells, bufalin plus gefitinib more strongly suppress EGFR‑PI3K/AKT‑mTOR components (EGFR, PI3K, AKT, mTOR, p70S6K) and tumor growth than either agent alone, indicating that intensified suppression of this pathway can reverse gefitinib resistance driven by EGFR T790M and downstream signaling.

Row13.Rationale: The PI3K/AKT pathway is a central RTK‑driven survival axis in lung cancer; abnormal activation is linked to tumor progression and resistance, and multiple studies show that continued PI3K/AKT activity—via on‑target mutations or bypass RTKs—limits responses to EGFR‑TKIs including gefitinib.

Row14.Rationale: Gefitinib‑resistant PC9GR cells exhibit elevated AXL, p‑AKT and p‑ERK versus parental PC9; enforced AXL overexpression increases gefitinib IC₅₀ ~3‑fold, while AXL knockdown restores sensitivity and suppresses PI3K/AKT and MAPK/ERK1/2, implicating AXL‑driven bypass signaling as a key resistance mechanism.

Row15.Rationale: Gefitinib‑resistant HCC827‑GR cells show marked PHLPP loss; PHLPP knockdown maintains AKT and ERK1/2 phosphorylation despite EGFR inhibition, reduces gefitinib‑induced apoptosis and increases IC₅₀, whereas PHLPP overexpression restores EGFR‑TKI sensitivity, indicating that PHLPP loss reactivates dual AKT/ERK signaling and confers resistance.

Row16.Rationale: Overexpression of HDGF in gefitinib‑sensitive PC‑9 cells induces resistance and sustains Akt and ERK activation despite EGFR blockade; HDGF knockdown in resistant H1975 cells enhances gefitinib sensitivity, and Akt or ERK inhibitors (MK2206, U0126) blunt HDGF‑mediated resistance, demonstrating HDGF‑driven bypass activation of EGFR downstream pathways.

Row17.Rationale: In intrinsically resistant EGFR‑mutant H1975 cells, IGF‑1R signaling is highly activated; IGF‑1R inhibition or knockdown enhances gefitinib‑induced growth inhibition and apoptosis via suppression of p‑AKT and upregulation of BIM, and metformin+gefitinib similarly suppresses IGF‑1R/AKT, implicating this pathway in primary gefitinib resistance.

Row18.Rationale: Multiple miRNAs modulate PI3K/AKT and EGFR‑TKI response: miR‑200c enhances gefitinib sensitivity by inhibiting PI3K/AKT; miR‑135a induces resistance through RAC1‑PI3K/AKT; let‑7c increases gefitinib sensitivity by suppressing RAS and inactivating PI3K/AKT and MEK/ERK, highlighting miRNA‑regulated PI3K/AKT circuitry as a determinant of gefitinib response.

Row19.Rationale: Gefitinib induces AMPK‑dependent autophagy in EGFR‑positive and EGFR‑wild‑type lung cancer cells; in A549 cells, this AMPK–autophagy activation accompanies apoptosis and growth inhibition, whereas resistant A549‑GR cells fail to mount this response, suggesting intact AMPK‑linked autophagy contributes to gefitinib cytotoxicity.

Row20.Rationale: Pharmacologic blockade of PI3K/AKT/mTOR by gefitinib or LY294002 in A549 cells promotes autophagy and apoptosis, and pathway inactivation alone is sufficient to trigger cell death, indicating that suppression of this axis with consequent autophagy induction is a key component of gefitinib sensitivity.

Row21.Pathway ID/Name: Shared PI3K/AKT node for EGFR, IGF‑1R, FGFR and other RTKs
Row21.Rationale: The PI3K/AKT pathway is activated downstream of multiple RTKs including EGFR, IGF‑1R and FGFR; in lung cancer, bypass activation of PI3K/AKT by non‑EGFR RTKs (e.g., IGF‑1R and HDGF‑driven signaling) maintains survival signaling when EGFR is inhibited, mediating intrinsic and acquired resistance to gefitinib.

Row22.Pathway ID/Name: EGFR–PI3K/AKT–mTOR axis under bufalin + gefitinib co‑targeting
Row22.Rationale: In H1975 xenografts and cells, bufalin plus gefitinib more strongly suppress EGFR‑PI3K/AKT‑mTOR components (EGFR, PI3K, AKT, mTOR, p70S6K) and tumor growth than either agent alone, indicating that intensified inhibition of this pathway can reverse gefitinib resistance driven by EGFR T790M and downstream signaling.
